# Raven Technical Reference Guide

This document provides comprehensive technical documentation for the Raven platform, covering API design, infrastructure, security, and operational procedures for enterprise deployments.

## API Endpoints

The Raven API follows RESTful conventions with versioned endpoints under the /api/v1 prefix. Core resources include documents, chunks, collections, and queries. Each endpoint supports standard HTTP methods for CRUD operations with JSON request and response bodies. The API implements content negotiation, supporting both application/json and application/msgpack formats. Pagination uses cursor-based tokens for consistent results across large datasets. All endpoints require authentication via Bearer tokens and return standardized error envelopes with machine-readable error codes. The API architecture supports both synchronous request-response patterns and asynchronous job submission for long-running operations like bulk document ingestion. Rate limit headers are included in every response to help clients implement adaptive throttling. OpenAPI 3.1 specifications are auto-generated from handler annotations and published at /api/v1/docs for interactive exploration.

## Authentication

Authentication is handled by Zitadel as the identity provider, issuing OIDC-compliant JWT access tokens. The API server validates tokens by checking the signature against the JWKS endpoint, verifying expiration claims, and confirming the audience matches the registered client. Service-to-service communication uses client credentials grants with scoped permissions. User sessions support refresh token rotation with configurable lifetimes. The authentication middleware extracts the org_id claim from the token to establish tenant context for row-level security. Multi-factor authentication is enforced for administrative operations including API key management, user provisioning, and configuration changes. Token introspection endpoints allow downstream services to validate tokens without direct access to the identity provider.

## Rate Limiting

Rate limiting is implemented using a sliding window algorithm backed by Valkey (Redis-compatible) counters. Each API key has configurable limits per endpoint group: read operations allow 1000 requests per minute, write operations allow 200, and search queries allow 500. When limits are exceeded, the API returns HTTP 429 with Retry-After headers indicating the reset window. Burst allowances permit short spikes up to 150% of the base rate. Rate limit state is shared across all API server instances through Valkey, ensuring consistent enforcement in horizontally scaled deployments. Administrative endpoints have separate, stricter limits to protect sensitive operations. Usage metrics are exported to the monitoring stack for capacity planning and abuse detection. Clients can query their current rate limit status via the /api/v1/rate-limit endpoint.

## Webhooks

The webhook system enables real-time event notification for document lifecycle events, search analytics, and system health changes. Webhook endpoints are registered per organization with configurable event filters and retry policies. Each delivery attempt includes an HMAC-SHA256 signature in the X-Webhook-Signature header, computed from the payload and a per-endpoint secret. Failed deliveries are retried with exponential backoff up to 5 attempts over 24 hours. The webhook payload includes the event type, timestamp, resource identifiers, and a snapshot of the changed data. Delivery logs are retained for 30 days and accessible through the admin API. Webhook endpoints must respond within 10 seconds or the delivery is marked as timed out. The architecture supports fan-out to multiple endpoints per event type for complex integration scenarios.

## Streaming Responses

The query API supports server-sent events for streaming retrieval-augmented generation responses. When the stream parameter is set to true, the server establishes an SSE connection and emits chunk references, relevance scores, and generated text tokens incrementally. Each SSE event includes a sequence number for client-side reassembly and gap detection. The streaming architecture uses Go channels internally, with a dedicated goroutine managing the SSE writer and backpressure handling. Clients can cancel streams mid-flight by closing the connection, which propagates context cancellation through the entire processing pipeline. Heartbeat events are sent every 15 seconds to maintain connection liveness through proxies and load balancers. The streaming endpoint shares the same authentication and rate limiting infrastructure as synchronous endpoints.

## Error Handling

All API errors follow a consistent envelope format with fields for error code, human-readable message, request ID, and optional detail objects. Error codes are hierarchical strings like document.not_found or search.index_unavailable that enable precise client-side handling. Validation errors include a per-field breakdown with JSONPath references to the invalid input locations. Internal server errors are logged with full stack traces and correlation IDs but return sanitized messages to clients. The error handling middleware captures panics, converts them to 500 responses, and triggers alerting. Retry-safe errors include an is_retryable boolean field to guide client retry logic. The architecture ensures that partial failures in batch operations return detailed per-item error reports rather than failing the entire request.

## SDK Libraries

Official SDK libraries are provided for Python, TypeScript, and Go, each generated from the OpenAPI specification with hand-tuned ergonomic wrappers. The Python SDK uses httpx for async support and includes Pydantic models for type-safe request and response handling. The TypeScript SDK provides full type inference and works in both Node.js and browser environments with tree-shakeable imports. The Go SDK follows idiomatic patterns with functional options for client configuration and context-aware methods throughout. All SDKs implement automatic retry with jittered exponential backoff, token refresh, and streaming response iteration. SDK versions are pinned to API versions and follow semantic versioning with changelog generation. Integration test suites run against a live sandbox environment in CI to catch compatibility regressions early.

## Deployment Architecture

Production deployments use a containerized architecture with Docker images for each service component. The API server, worker pool, and background job processor run as separate containers orchestrated by Docker Compose for single-node deployments or Kubernetes for multi-node clusters. Each container follows the twelve-factor app methodology with configuration injected through environment variables. The deployment architecture supports blue-green deployments with health check gates to ensure zero-downtime upgrades. Database migrations run as init containers that complete before application containers start. Static assets and generated documentation are served through a CDN layer. The architecture is designed to run on resource-constrained edge nodes including Raspberry Pi devices for on-premises enterprise deployments.

## Monitoring and Observability

The observability stack uses OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing, Prometheus-format metrics, and structured logging with slog. Traces propagate through HTTP headers and gRPC metadata across service boundaries using W3C Trace Context format. Key metrics include request latency histograms, search quality scores, embedding throughput, and cache hit ratios. Log entries are structured JSON with correlation IDs, tenant context, and severity levels following the syslog standard. The monitoring architecture feeds into OpenObserve for centralized analysis and alerting. Custom dashboards track business metrics like query success rates, document processing pipeline throughput, and per-tenant usage patterns. Health check endpoints expose readiness and liveness probes for orchestrator integration.

## Horizontal Scaling

The platform scales horizontally by adding API server instances behind a load balancer and worker instances consuming from shared task queues. Stateless API servers enable simple horizontal scaling with session affinity not required. The Asynq task queue backed by Valkey distributes embedding jobs, document processing tasks, and webhook deliveries across worker pools. Auto-scaling policies trigger based on queue depth and API latency percentiles. Database connection pooling through PgBouncer prevents connection exhaustion under high concurrency. The scaling architecture supports geographic distribution with read replicas for search-heavy workloads. Embedding computation can be offloaded to GPU-equipped nodes while keeping the API tier on cost-effective CPU instances.

## Database Schema

The PostgreSQL schema centers on a normalized design with tables for organizations, documents, chunks, embeddings, and response_cache. The chunks table includes a tsvector column for BM25 full-text search and a vector(1536) column for semantic similarity via pgvector. Foreign key relationships enforce referential integrity from chunks to documents to organizations. Partial indexes on frequently filtered columns like org_id and document status reduce index bloat. The schema uses PostgreSQL native partitioning on the chunks table by org_id for large multi-tenant deployments. JSONB columns store flexible metadata without schema migrations. Check constraints enforce business rules at the database level including valid enum values and positive token counts.

## Database Migrations

Schema migrations use golang-migrate with sequential numbered files in the migrations directory. Each migration pair consists of an up.sql and down.sql file ensuring full reversibility. Migrations run automatically during deployment as Kubernetes init containers or Docker entrypoint scripts. The migration framework supports transactional DDL, wrapping each migration in a BEGIN/COMMIT block for atomic application. Migration state is tracked in a schema_migrations table with dirty flags for failed migration detection. New migrations are generated using a CLI tool that creates timestamped file pairs. The testing infrastructure runs all migrations forward and backward in CI to verify reversibility. Migration files follow a naming convention of NNNNNN_description.up.sql for clarity.

## Testing Strategy

The test suite spans unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests organized by package. Unit tests use testify for assertions and mock interfaces for external dependencies. Integration tests run against a dockerized PostgreSQL instance with pgvector extensions loaded, verifying actual SQL queries and search behavior. End-to-end tests exercise the full API through HTTP requests against a test server with seeded data. Test fixtures provide deterministic sample documents, pre-computed embeddings, and expected search results. The testing architecture uses build tags to separate fast unit tests from slower integration tests. Code coverage thresholds are enforced in CI with minimum 80% line coverage for core packages. Fuzz tests target parsing and input validation paths to catch edge cases.

## CI/CD Pipeline

The CI pipeline uses GitHub Actions with separate workflows for testing, linting, and deployment. Pull requests trigger parallel jobs for Go tests, Python worker tests, and frontend Playwright tests. The linting stage runs golangci-lint for Go code and ruff for Python code with zero-tolerance for warnings. Docker images are built using multi-stage Dockerfiles with layer caching for fast rebuilds. The deployment pipeline promotes images through staging and production environments with automated smoke tests at each gate. Release tagging follows semantic versioning with automated changelog generation from conventional commit messages. Artifact signing ensures image provenance and prevents supply chain tampering. The pipeline architecture uses reusable workflow templates for consistency across repository services.

## Security Model

The security model implements defense in depth with authentication, authorization, encryption, and audit logging at every layer. All data in transit uses TLS 1.3 with strong cipher suites and certificate pinning for service-to-service communication. Data at rest is encrypted using AES-256-GCM with keys managed through envelope encryption. Row-level security policies in PostgreSQL enforce tenant isolation at the database layer, preventing cross-tenant data access even in the event of application-level bugs. Input validation uses allowlist patterns to prevent injection attacks across SQL, command, and template contexts. Security headers including CSP, HSTS, and X-Frame-Options are set on all HTTP responses. Dependency scanning runs daily with automated pull requests for security patches.

## Structured Logging

Application logs use Go slog with JSON output format for machine parseability. Log entries include standard fields: timestamp, level, message, request_id, org_id, and service_name. The logging architecture uses a multi-handler pipeline with samber/slog-multi for routing logs to different destinations based on severity. Debug-level logs are sampled at 10% in production to reduce volume while preserving diagnostic capability. Sensitive fields like access tokens, passwords, and PII are automatically redacted by a custom slog handler before emission. Log correlation uses the W3C Trace Context trace_id to link log entries with distributed traces. Error-level logs trigger immediate alerting through the monitoring stack. Structured logging enables powerful querying in OpenObserve for incident investigation and trend analysis.

## Configuration Management

Application configuration uses a layered approach: compiled defaults, configuration files, environment variables, and runtime feature flags. The Go server reads YAML configuration files with Viper, with environment variable overrides following the RAVEN_SECTION_KEY naming convention. Feature flags are stored in the database and cached locally with configurable refresh intervals. Configuration validation runs at startup, failing fast with descriptive error messages for missing or invalid values. Sensitive configuration values like database passwords and API keys are loaded from encrypted environment files using dotenvx. The configuration architecture supports hot-reload for non-critical settings without requiring service restarts. Default values are chosen for secure-by-default behavior, requiring explicit opt-in for development-only features.

## Environment Variables

Key environment variables control database connections, service discovery, and feature toggles. DATABASE_URL specifies the PostgreSQL connection string with sslmode, pool_size, and statement_cache parameters. VALKEY_URL configures the Valkey connection for task queues and rate limiting. EMBEDDING_ENDPOINT sets the URL for the embedding service, supporting both OpenAI-compatible APIs and local model servers. LIVEKIT_URL and LIVEKIT_API_KEY configure real-time communication for collaborative features. SEAWEEDFS_MASTER sets the object storage endpoint for document file management. LOG_LEVEL controls the minimum severity for log emission with valid values of debug, info, warn, and error. RAVEN_ENV distinguishes between development, staging, and production environments for conditional behavior.

## Docker Setup

The Docker Compose configuration defines services for the API server, Python worker, PostgreSQL with pgvector, Valkey, SeaweedFS, and Zitadel identity provider. Each service specifies health checks, resource limits, and dependency ordering through depends_on conditions. The PostgreSQL service uses a custom Dockerfile that installs the pgvector extension and configures shared_preload_libraries for optimal full-text search performance. Volume mounts persist database data, object storage files, and configuration between container restarts. A dedicated Docker network isolates service communication with DNS-based service discovery. The development Compose file adds hot-reload configurations with volume mounts for source code. Multi-architecture images support both amd64 and arm64 platforms for deployment flexibility on edge hardware including Raspberry Pi nodes.

## Kubernetes Deployment

The Kubernetes manifests provide production-grade deployment configurations with Helm charts for parameterized installation. StatefulSets manage PostgreSQL and Valkey instances with persistent volume claims for data durability. Deployments handle stateless API servers and workers with horizontal pod autoscalers based on CPU utilization and custom metrics. Ingress resources configure TLS termination and path-based routing with cert-manager for automated certificate provisioning. ConfigMaps and Secrets store application configuration with external-secrets-operator integration for vault-backed secret management. Network policies restrict inter-pod communication to explicitly allowed paths. The Kubernetes architecture supports both self-managed clusters and managed offerings like EKS, GKE, and AKS with minimal configuration changes. Pod disruption budgets ensure availability during voluntary disruptions like node drains and cluster upgrades.
